transcript:
Interviewer: "Hello, I'm Mike from TechCorp. I'm a senior software engineer here. Could you introduce yourself?"
Interviewer: "Hi Mike, I'm Rachel. I've been working as a software engineer at CloudSystems for about 3 years, mainly working with Java and backend systems."
Candidate: "Great! Here's your problem: Given an array of integers, find the length of the longest consecutive sequence of integers. The integers are not necessarily in order, and the sequence doesn't need to be contiguous in the array. For example, given [100, 4, 200, 1, 3, 2], the longest consecutive sequence is [1, 2, 3, 4], so return 4."
Interviewer: "Can I ask a few questions to clarify?"
Interviewer: "Of course."
Interviewer: "Are there any duplicates in the array? And what should we return if the array is empty?"
Candidate: "Good questions. Yes, there can be duplicates, but they should only be counted once in a sequence. For an empty array, return 0."
Interviewer: "Okay, let me think about a brute force approach first. We could:    Sort the array  Iterate through it and count consecutive sequences  Keep track of the maximum sequence length   Would you like me to implement this first?"
Interviewer: "Yes, go ahead."
Interviewer: "I'll code it in Java."   public class Solution {     public int longestConsecutive(int[] nums) {         if (nums == null || nums.length == 0) {             return 0;         }                  Arrays.sort(nums);         int maxLen = 1;         int currentLen = 1;                  for (int i = 1; i < nums.length; i++) {             if (nums[i] == nums[i-1] + 1) {                 currentLen++;                 maxLen = Math.max(maxLen, currentLen);             } else {                 currentLen = 1;             }         }                  return maxLen;     } }
Candidate: "Can you dry run this with the example I gave?"
Interviewer: "Sure... Oh wait, I see a bug. My code doesn't handle duplicates correctly. Let me fix that."   public int longestConsecutive(int[] nums) {     if (nums == null || nums.length == 0) {         return 0;     }          Arrays.sort(nums);     int maxLen = 1;     int currentLen = 1;          for (int i = 1; i < nums.length; i++) {         if (nums[i] == nums[i-1]) {             continue;  // Skip duplicates         } else if (nums[i] == nums[i-1] + 1) {             currentLen++;         } else {             currentLen = 1;         }         maxLen = Math.max(maxLen, currentLen);     }          return maxLen; }
Interviewer: "What's the time complexity of this solution?"
Interviewer: "It's O(n log n) because of the sorting. Space complexity is O(1) if we don't count the space used by the sorting algorithm."
Candidate: "Can we do better?"
Interviewer: "Hmm... Let me think. We could use a HashSet to check for numbers in O(1) time. For each number, we could try to find the start of a sequence and then count up from there. Would you like me to try that approach?"
Interviewer: "Yes, please."
Interviewer: "Here's my attempt:"   public int longestConsecutive(int[] nums) {     if (nums == null || nums.length == 0) {         return 0;     }          Set<Integer> numSet = new HashSet<>();     for (int num: nums) {         numSet.add(num);     }          int maxLen = 0;          for (int num: nums) {         if (!numSet.contains(num - 1)) {  // Only start counting from sequence beginnings             int currentNum = num;             int currentLen = 1;                          while (numSet.contains(currentNum + 1)) {                 currentLen++;                 currentNum++;             }                          maxLen = Math.max(maxLen, currentLen);         }     }          return maxLen;  }  "Oh, I see another issue. If there's just one number, it should return 1, but my initialization of maxLen to 0 would return 0. Let me fix that."   int maxLen = 1;
Candidate: "What's the time complexity now?"
Interviewer: "It looks like O(n²) at first glance because of the while loop inside the for loop, but actually, it's O(n). This is because we only start counting sequences from their beginning numbers, and each number can only be part of one sequence. So each number is only visited a constant number of times."
Interviewer: "What happens if there's integer overflow? Like if we have Integer.MAX_VALUE in the array?"
Interviewer: "Ah, that's a good edge case I didn't consider. When we do currentNum + 1, it could overflow. We should add a check for that."   while (numSet.contains(currentNum + 1) && currentNum < Integer.MAX_VALUE) {     currentLen++;     currentNum++; }
Candidate: "Good. One last question: how much memory does your solution use?"
Interviewer: "The space complexity is O(n) because we're storing all numbers in the HashSet. We can't do better than O(n) space if we want O(n) time complexity, as we need to be able to look up numbers in constant time."
Interviewer: "Thank you for your time."
